query: does serotonin get transported by platelets

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Platelet -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: As a result of degranulation, ADP, TXA, serotonin, fibrinogen, and P-selectin are secreted into the plasma. ADP and TXA are especially important in the activation of platelets. Released ADP binds to P2Y and P2Y receptors on the platelet surface, further increasing signal transduction and activation in the platelet, while TXA binds to thromboxane prostanoid receptors, increasing activation of nearby platelets. Both of these are critical in the recruitment of other platelets to form a large platelet plug. Serotonin acts in a similar, but less potent, way on 5HT receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Serotonin -- Clinical Significance -- Clotting
retrieved_docs.content: When serotonin is released into the blood, it is often absorbed by platelets rather than remaining free serotonin. The effect serotonin has on the platelets is similar to those produced by the interaction of platelet factor 2 and platelets. Serotonin accelerates the metabolism of fibrinogen to fibrin. This action causes platelet aggregation, leading to vasoconstriction, resulting in a reduction in the blood flow and an increase in clot formation; this is 1 of the earliest defined functions of serotonin. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Serotonin -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: In the central nervous system, serotonin processing occurs in several ways. Upon neuronal depolarization, serotonin is released into the synaptic cleft. It can bind to either postsynaptic serotonin receptors (5-HT receptors) or presynaptic serotonin autoreceptors. [1] Serotonin binding to the autoreceptor acts as negative feedback against the further release of serotonin into the synaptic cleft. The highly selective serotonin transporter (SERT), located on the presynaptic membrane, removes serotonin from the synaptic cleft. [7] Once transported into the presynaptic neuron, serotonin is recycled back into presynaptic vesicles, protected from metabolism. Metabolism by monoamine oxidase (MAO) occurs within the cytosol of the neuron. An alternate pathway for serotonin exists in the pineal gland; it converts to melatonin. [2] Serotonin originating from enterochromaffin cells is released into the portal circulation and undergoes rapid elimination from the plasma by way of uptake into platelets and liver metabolism. Serotonin transporters on the platelet membrane and enterochromaffin cells function to uptake serotonin into those cells. Serotonin escapes uptake, and liver metabolism reaches the lung, which then undergoes metabolism. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Serotonin -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: When excessive serotonin is released from the enterochromaffin cell, it is frequently introduced to the bloodstream, where it interacts with blood platelets. The platelets absorb the serotonin and store it until a clot forms. However, once a clot forms, the serotonin is re-released in the blood, where it can regulate hemostasis and blood clotting. [8] At elevated levels, serotonin functions by contracting vascular smooth muscle cells, leading to vasoconstriction. However, at lower levels, serotonin facilitates the endothelial class to release nitric oxide, leading to vasodilation. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Platelet Transfusion -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Sol-gel zone: The Sol-gel zone comprises microtubules and microfilaments, which are vital components for platelet structure and support. This region is responsible for various shape changes on activation during hemostasis and ex vivo storage. Organelles are embedded within this matrix. Organelle zone: This region contains alpha and dense granules, mitochondria, glycogen, lysosomes, and peroxisomes. Alpha granules store fibrinogen, factor V, von Willebrand factor, platelet-derived growth factor, cytokines, chemokines, transforming growth factor beta 1, and vascular endothelial growth factor. Dense granules store calcium, adenine nucleotides, polyphosphate, pyrophosphate, serotonin, and histamine. [6] Mitochondria are the powerhouses of platelets. Glycogen provides carbohydrates for fuel. Lysosomes and peroxisomes play vital roles in cellular defense mechanisms (see Image . An Overview of Platelet Structure).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hemostasis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet Activation. The platelets that have adhered undergo very specific changes. They release their cytoplasmic granules that include ADP, thromboxane A2, serotonin, and multiple other activation factors. They also undergo a transformation of their shape into a pseudopodal shape which in-turn leads to release reactions of various chemokines. P2Y1 receptors help in the conformational changes in platelets. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Platelet Activation -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: As platelets continue to degranulate and their activation continues along a positive feedback loop, adenosine diphosphate (ADP) released from dense granules bind to P2Y1 and P2Y12 receptors located on the platelet’s membrane, inducing the expression of the GPIIb/IIIa receptor. This newly inserted platelet receptor, along with thromboxane A2 (TXA2) produced by platelet enzyme cyclooxygenase, allows for platelet aggregation. Though its role in hemostasis is currently under investigation, serotonin released from dense granules appears to function as another mediator of platelet aggregation while also promoting further vasoconstriction. Fibrinogen from alpha granules acts as a link between GPIIb/IIIa receptors on different platelets. Once platelets have linked together, a temporary platelet plug has formed, which is later fortified by the coagulation cascade of secondary hemostasis.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Platelet -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The outer membrane of platelets is critical for its function in hemostasis. Several receptors that are expressed on its surface either constitutively or after activation allow for adhesion to endothelial surfaces as well as aggregation with other platelets. Inside the platelet, alpha granules and dense granules are present, which contain specific compounds that are critical for a variety of functions. Alpha granules are more numerous and contain compounds like P-selectin, GPIIb/IIIa, GPIb, von Willebrand factor (vWF), factors V, IX, and XIII, and others. Dense granules contain some of these compounds but are principally responsible for storing calcium, potassium, serotonin, and important nucleotides such as ATP and ADP. [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Platelets -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: As described above, platelets are anuclear but do contain ribonucleic acid, ribosomes, mitochondria, and various granules, which are important in enacting platelet function. [2] There are three distinct types of platelet granule α-granules: the most abundant and largest secretory granules, containing most of the platelet factors involved in hemostasis, including p-selectin, von Willebrand Factor, and fibrinogen [4] Dense granules: the smallest granules, which are visible on electron microscopy as dense bodies and contain adenosine diphosphate and serotonin, as well as high levels of calcium Lysosomes: contain hydrolytic enzymes, including acid phosphatase and arylsulfatase [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Clotting Mechanism -- Mechanism -- Primary Hemostasis
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet activation is mediated via thrombin by two mechanisms. Thrombin directly activates platelets via proteolytic cleavage by binding the protease-activated receptor. Thrombin also stimulates platelet granule release which includes serotonin, platelet activating factor, and Adenosine Diphosphate (ADP). ADP is an important physiological agonist which is stored specifically in the dense granules of platelets. When ADP is released, it binds to P2Y1 and P2Y12 receptors on platelet membranes. P2Y1 induces the pseudopod shape change and aids in platelet aggregation. P2Y12 plays a major role in inducing the clotting cascade. When ADP binds to its receptors, it induces Gp IIb/IIIa complex expression at the platelet membrane surface. The Gp IIb/IIIa complex is a calcium-dependent collagen receptor which is necessary for platelet-to-endothelial adherence and platelet-to-platelet aggregation. Simultaneously, platelets synthesize Thromboxane A2 (TXA2). TXA2 further intensifies vasoconstriction and platelet aggregation (next step in the primary hemostasis process). The process of platelet activation readies the local environment for platelet aggregation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Serotonin -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular: Serotonin has positive inotropic and chronotropic effects by increasing intracellular calcium in cardiac myocytes, which can trigger tachyarrhythmias. [15] Serotonin can be stored in platelet granules and plays a role in platelet aggregation. Serotonin can contribute to vasodilation, in the setting of intact endothelium and vasoconstriction, in the setting of damaged endothelium. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Coagulopathies -- Diagnostic Tests -- Antiplatelet Antibodies
retrieved_docs.content: Functional assays: Functional methods can identify antibodies that activate the patient's platelets. They are performed only in a few specialized centers and used to confirm heparin-induced thrombocytopenia in cases where the result of immunoassay methods is doubtful or a discrepancy between clinical presentations. The immunoassay method is evident, for example, negative ELISA in a patient with high clinical suspicion and a high 4T score). Serotonin release assay : The serotonin release assay is the gold standard for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia but is time-consuming and available only in a few specialized laboratories. This method is based on platelet activation of normal donors through serotonin release in the patient's serum and heparin. Platelets from normal donors are radio-labeled with 14C-serotonin and incubated with the patient's serum in therapeutic and higher doses of heparin. Positive results indicate the release of 14C-serotonin when therapeutic doses of heparin (0.1 units/mL) are used but not higher doses (100 units/mL). This discrepancy occurs because the binding of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia antibodies is specific to a particular ratio of heparin to PF4. Heparin-induced platelet activation: In this method, serum or plasma from the patient with suspected heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is added to platelet-rich plasma from healthy donors, and the activation of platelets is measured through platelet aggregation in the absence and presence of low and high doses of heparin. The use of washed platelets increases the sensitivity of the method, making it the most widely used specialized confirmation method. A positive test shows minimal platelet activation in the absence of heparin and with high doses of heparin with pronounced activation in the presence of low-dose heparin.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Thromboxane A2 -- Mechanism -- Platelet Degranulation
retrieved_docs.content: A variety of mediators can induce platelet degranulation. TxA2 induces platelet degranulation via the Gq/PLC-beta pathway. Secretion from platelets is an important mechanism that amplifies platelet activation and results in the release of mediators that act on the vessel wall and other blood cells. It occurs in 2 waves; the first consists of the release of dense core granules and alpha-granules, followed by lysosome release. Dense granules contain small molecules such as nucleotides (ADP, ATP) or serotonin, whereas alpha granules contain various proteins, including growth factors, chemokines, adhesive molecules such as Von Willebrand Factor (vWF), and coagulation factors. The central role of the Gq/PLC-beta pathway in agonist-induced platelet granule secretion is supported by the finding that various platelet activators failed to induce secretion in platelets lacking Galpha-q and by the fact that secretion in response to TxA2 is reduced in PLC-beta–deficient platelets. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Serotonin Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine or 5-HT) is a monoamine neurotransmitter with multifaceted cellular and physiological functions in humans. Most serotonin is produced by intestinal enterochromaffin cells and in the peripheral nervous system, where it regulates gastrointestinal motility, uterine contractions, vasoconstriction, and bronchoconstriction. [6] The raphe nuclei in the brain stem produce serotonin in the central nervous system, CNS. In the CNS, serotonin modulates attention, behavior, and thermoregulation. Additionally, serotonin is produced in the skin's Merkel cells, pulmonary neuroendocrine cells, and taste receptor cells of the tongue. Further, serotonin is stored in blood platelets and promotes aggregation. Notably, platelets lack enzymes to synthesize serotonin independently and rely on serotonin uptake from the plasma to store serotonin. [7] Serotonin production is 90% from gastrointestinal enterochromaffin cells, 8% from platelets, and 2% from the CNS. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Serotonin -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Serotonin is a monoamine neurotransmitter that plays a role in several complex biological functions. [1] [2] Its common abbreviation is 5-HT because of its chemical name: 5-hydroxytryptamine. The attribution for the discovery of serotonin is that it occurred serendipitously by Vittorio Erspmarer during his attempt to purify extract from enterochromaffin cells in 1935. [3] Serotonin forms from the hydroxylation (i.e., the addition of -OH group) and decarboxylation of the tryptophan amino acid. The highest serotonin concentration is in the gastrointestinal tract's enterochromaffin cells, with small amounts in the central nervous system and platelets. Serotonin induces changes in the cell by its action on the serotonergic receptors, which are coupled to different G proteins mediating intracellular changes. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Serotonin Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The serotonin signaling pathway in the CNS begins at the production source, in the brainstem's raphe nuclei. The raphe nuclei are centered in the reticular formation, and their axons extend to other brain areas. The rostral group of nuclei extends into cortical and subcortical structures, and these pathways are anticipated to regulate wakefulness, attention, mood, sexual behavior, thermoregulation, and appetite. The caudal raphe nuclei extend into the spinal regions and regulate motor tone and nociception. [9] The peripheral serotonin signaling pathway generally begins with platelet activation, leading to serotonin secretion from stores back into the plasma, which has downstream effects on target organ serotonin receptors. [10]

retrieved_docs.content: as a parent compound for tropisetron, demonstrating a shared chemical backbone despite their distinct therapeutic indications. Together, these relationships show how prostaglandins integrate biochemical pathways, drug classification systems, and multiple therapeutic areas, while serotonin‑3 antagonists exemplify the diversification of carboxylic‑acid‑based structures into both analgesic and antiemetic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1925
retrieved_docs.content: Carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product is a chemical class that includes both phenyl salicylate and tropisetron. Phenyl salicylate, a salicylate ester, and tropisetron, a 5‑HT₃ receptor antagonist used as an antiemetic, each possess a carboxylic acid functional group or a derivative of it, linking them to this broader product category. Recognizing that these two distinct compounds share the carboxylic acid moiety helps explain similarities in their physicochemical properties, formulation considerations, and potential metabolic pathways, which is important for pharmaceutical development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 129
retrieved_docs.content: Alosetron, a selective 5‑HT₃ receptor antagonist indicated for gastrointestinal disorders, carries a contraindication in patients with thrombophlebitis (التهاب الوريد الخثاري), a condition that is clinically related to retinal thrombophlebitis (التهاب الوريد الخثاري بالشبكية). Understanding these hierarchical and therapeutic relationships aids clinicians in selecting appropriate cardiovascular, respiratory, allergic, and gastrointestinal therapies while avoiding contraindicated agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1339
retrieved_docs.content: BLOOD AND BLOOD FORMING ORGAN DRUGS constitute a broad therapeutic class that includes antithrombotic agents; antithrombotic agents are a specific subclass of these drugs and are used to inhibit platelet aggregation and coagulation, thereby preventing the formation of pathological blood clots in various clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: 75—serve as plasma expanders and oxygen carriers; dextran 40 is used for hypovolemic (cardiogenic) shock and prophylaxis of venous thrombosis and other thromboembolic events, while dextran 70 and dextran 75 are used to treat shock and prevent venous thrombosis. All dextran forms and hydroxyethyl starch are contraindicated in thrombotic disease and must not be co‑administered with pimozid, which itself is contraindicated with sertraline. Cortisol succinate is an additional therapeutic option for cardiogenic shock. Collectively, these entities and relationships guide clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies, recognizing contraindications, and anticipating adverse reactions across gastrointestinal, lipid‑lowering, cardiovascular, and blood‑substitute domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1946
retrieved_docs.content: Tropisetron is classified as a serotonin (5‑HT₃) antagonist, belonging to the drug class that blocks the 5‑HT₃ receptor. Serotonin (5‑HT₃) antagonists, including tropisetron, are used clinically to inhibit serotonin‑mediated signaling that can trigger nausea, vomiting, and related gastrointestinal symptoms, thereby improving patient comfort and outcomes in settings such as chemotherapy, postoperative care, and radiation therapy.

O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin is listed among blood substitutes and plasma‑protein fractions. Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate, members of the ATC class for peptic ulcer and gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease, treat gastrointestinal disturbances such as اضطراب معدي and diarrhea (الإسهال); they are contraindicated in thrombotic disease and can mitigate diarrhea induced by niacinamide. Other ATC class members for ulcer‑related conditions include pirenzepin and gefarnát. Alosetron hydrochloride, a serotonin‑2 antagonist used for certain gastrointestinal disorders, is contraindicated in thrombophlebitis (التهاب الوريد الخثاري) and may cause diarrhea. Thrombophlebitis is linked to its retinal manifestation (التهاب الوريد الخثاري بالشبكية). Cisaprid, a prokinetic agent, has a contraindicated‑class relationship with dextran 40, dextran 70 and dextran 75. Pimozid is contraindicated when combined

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821
retrieved_docs.content: Tirofiban hydrochloride, a glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor antagonist employed to inhibit platelet aggregation in acute coronary syndromes, is contraindicated in patients who have vascular dilation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية). Use of this antiplatelet agent in the presence of excessive vasodilation may predispose to bleeding or hemodynamic instability, so clinicians should avoid prescribing tirofiban hydrochloride to individuals with this condition.

Propulsives also include the pro‑kinetic agents cisaprid and metoclopramide; cisaprid is contraindicated when co‑administered with Dextran 70 or Dextran 75. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2949
retrieved_docs.content: Serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders constitute a class of drugs indicated for the treatment of functional gastrointestinal disorders, and alosetron is identified as a specific member of this drug class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 696
retrieved_docs.content: Cisaprid is contraindicated for use with the dextran solutions dextran 40, dextran 70, and dextran 75, indicating that these high‑molecular‑weight glucose polymers should not be co‑administered with cisaprid. Dextran 40, dextran 70, and dextran 75 each have the therapeutic property of helping to prevent venous thrombosis (التخثر الوريدي), making them useful agents in the prophylaxis of clot formation. However, all three dextran formulations are also contraindicated for use with the anti‑emetic pimozid, reflecting a drug‑interaction risk between dextran solutions and pimozid. In addition, pimozid itself is contraindicated for use with the selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor sertraline (sertralin), further limiting the combination of these medications. This network of contraindications highlights the importance of avoiding concurrent administration of cisaprid with dextran 40/70/75, avoiding dextran 40/70/75 with pimozid, and avoiding pimozid with sertraline, while recognizing the beneficial antithrombotic effect of dextran solutions when used appropriately.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2808
retrieved_docs.content: Tirofiban, a glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor used to prevent platelet aggregation, is contraindicated in patients who have the condition of تمدد الأوعية الدموية (vascular dilation). This contraindication underscores the need for clinicians to evaluate for vascular dilation before initiating tirofiban therapy, ensuring that the medication is not administered to individuals for whom it may be harmful.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 121
retrieved_docs.content: Noradrenaline, the active ingredient of the product .GAMMA‑AMINOBUTYRIC ACID 6 [hp_X] Neuro‑Pro T007, is used together with the vasopressor metaraminol for the treatment of shock. Dextran 40, a low‑molecular‑weight fraction of the polysaccharide dextran, is also indicated for shock and is reported to help prevent venous thrombosis and unspecified thromboembolic embolism; its related fractions dextran 70 and dextran 75 share these plasma‑expanding and anti‑thrombotic properties and may be employed in shock, including cardiogenic shock. All three dextran fractions belong to the “Blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions” group, which additionally includes O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin and hydroxyethyl starch. Dextran 40 and hydroxyethyl starch are contraindicated in patients with thrombotic disease (الاعتلال الخثري). Drug‑drug contraindications are noted: dextran 40 is contraindicated with cisaprid and pimozid; cisaprid is specifically contraindicated with dextran 40, dextran 70, and dextran 75; pimozid is contraindicated with dextran 40, dextran 70, dextran 75, and also with sertraline. Cortisol succinate is another medication that may be used in shock management. The Dutch term “Arteriële embolie en trombose” designates aortic and peripheral arterial embolism or thrombosis, a subcategory of arterial embolism and thrombosis, which is associated with an unspecified thrombotic embolism morphology (انصمام خثاري غير محدد). Recognizing these therapeutic indications, contraindications, and classifications assists clinicians in treating shock, preventing venous and arterial thrombotic complications, and avoiding adverse drug‑disease and drug‑drug interactions.

Ibopamine is not classified as a phenol‑type substance, droxidopa is not an amino‑acid derivative, whereas tiropramide is an amino‑acid derivative. Myo‑endo‑pericardial and valve disorders are closely linked to heart failure, in which several agents are contraindicated: cisapride, cisapride monohydrate, fingolimod (including its hydrochloride and lauryl‑sulfate salts) and ketamine hydrochloride. Cisapride also should not be co‑administered with dextran 40, dextran 70, dextran 75, furosemide or fluconazole (flukonazol); dextran 40 is a polysaccharide plasma expander used to manage shock and to prevent venous thrombosis and other thromboembolic events. Pimozid is contraindicated with dextran 40, dextran 70, dextran 75 and sertraline, and it also interacts adversely with fluconazole. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1123
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran is a family of polysaccharide solutions that includes dextran 40, dextran 70, and dextran 75. All three dextran formulations are used in the management of shock, where they help restore intravascular volume, and they also have a role in preventing venous thrombosis (التخثر الوريدي). However, each dextran product is contraindicated in patients receiving pimozid, reflecting a drug‑interaction risk that must be considered when selecting volume expanders. Pimozid itself is contraindicated with sertralin (sertraline), indicating an additional precaution for patients on serotonergic antidepressants. Besides dextran, cortisol succinate is another agent that may be employed to treat shock. In this context, shock is classified as صَدْمَة قَلْبِيَّة المَنْشَأ, or cardiogenic shock, underscoring the importance of careful therapeutic choices to support cardiac output while avoiding harmful drug interactions.
